content.text: When a large language model generates text, it produces a probability distribution over its entire vocabulary at every generation step. Log probabilities — logprobs — are the natural logarithm of those per-token probabilities, and they are among the most actionable diagnostic and control signals available to application developers building AI systems.
At each generation step, the model's final transformer layer outputs a vector of raw unnormalized scores called
— one score per vocabulary token. Applying the
function converts these logits into a proper probability distribution: every entry is non-negative and the entries sum to one. The
of a token is the natural logarithm of its softmax probability.
. A logprob of 0 means the model assigned the token a probability of 1 (absolute certainty). A logprob of
corresponds to a probability of about 60 %. A logprob of
means the model considered the token very unlikely (under 0.1 %).

In the context of inference APIs, the term 'logprobs' refers to the log-probabilities of the tokens chosen during generation, and optionally the top-k alternative tokens at each position. The OpenAI Chat Completions API exposes these via the

Greedy decoding — always selecting the highest-probability token — produces fluent text but reveals nothing about how confident the model actually was. Two outputs that look equally fluent could be generated with very different confidence levels: one where every chosen token had logprob near zero, and another where the model was nearly 50-50 between two alternatives at each step.
Access to logprobs provides three categories of value for production AI systems:

An agent that sums or averages the logprobs of its own output can estimate how 'sure' the generation was. Very negative means signal that the model was interpolating between multiple plausible completions — a strong cue to seek human confirmation, regenerate, or escalate to a more capable model.

Summing logprobs across a completion enables computing perplexity, the standard measure of how well a model fits a piece of text. Perplexity is essential for evaluation, fine-tuning diagnostics, and comparing candidate responses.

By reading the logprob of a specific label token given a shared prompt prefix, developers can implement classifiers and re-rankers directly using the generation model with no gradient updates or separate classifiers.

These capabilities are especially critical in agentic pipelines where errors compound across multiple steps. Knowing which generation steps were uncertain lets an orchestrator insert verification, request clarification, or choose a safer fallback path before committing to an action.
Logprobs are a byproduct of the forward pass. Requesting them adds negligible latency compared to the base generation cost — the log-softmax is already computed internally.
Working with log-probabilities avoids the floating-point underflow that occurs when multiplying many small probabilities together. This is the primary reason the field works in log-space.
Joint probability of a sequence is a product of per-token probabilities, which becomes a sum of per-token logprobs in log-space. Sequence-level scoring is therefore trivially efficient.
The mathematical meaning is identical across all transformer-based LLMs. API surface differs by provider, but the underlying quantity is the same.
Enables techniques impossible with text alone.
Beam search, minimum Bayes risk decoding, calibrated temperature tuning, and uncertainty-gated routing all require per-token probability estimates.
The Anthropic Claude API does not expose logprobs. OpenAI's API supports them via
. Access depends on provider and deployment mode.
at 20 alternatives per position. The full vocabulary distribution (often 50,000–200,000 tokens) is never returned, so rare-token analysis is incomplete.
on a long output substantially increases response payload. For streaming applications this can increase time-to-first-token slightly.
A model can be highly confident and factually wrong. High logprob (narrow distribution) does not guarantee accuracy — only that the model's internal distribution was peaked.

The logprobs returned by inference APIs reflect probabilities after temperature scaling. At temperature 0 the distribution is deterministic; at temperature > 1 it is flatter. Always use temperature 0 or 1 when using logprobs as calibrated confidence signals.
The complete pipeline from prompt to logprob has four stages. Understanding each stage prevents common misinterpretations.

The input text is split into subword tokens from the model's vocabulary (BPE or SentencePiece). Each token maps to an integer ID. The tokenization affects which probability is assigned to which character sequence — a single word may be split into multiple tokens, each carrying its own logprob.
The token IDs are embedded and processed by the transformer stack. The final hidden state
is projected by an unembedding matrix
to produce the raw logit vector:
Rather than computing softmax and then taking the log — which is numerically unstable for extreme logit values — modern implementations use the log-sum-exp (LSE) trick:

Subtracting the maximum before exponentiation keeps all terms near 1, preventing overflow from large positive logits and underflow from large negative ones. This is why numerical implementations of softmax and log-softmax always subtract the maximum.

The sampler draws a token from the distribution (or takes argmax for greedy decoding at temperature 0). The framework packages the logprob of the chosen token and the top-k alternatives into the API response.
The diagram below illustrates the full token probability pipeline and the downstream applications that logprobs enable.

Each generation step is independent: the model runs a full forward pass (using the KV cache for efficiency) and produces a fresh distribution over the entire vocabulary for the next position. The logprob at position
reflects model uncertainty at that specific position given everything generated before it — it is not a global confidence score for the entire response.
that asks for human confirmation when output confidence falls below a threshold — essential for autonomous agents that take irreversible actions.
to evaluate or compare model responses without a separate scoring model — useful in A/B tests and model selection.
over a fixed label set by comparing the logprob of each label token given a shared prompt prefix.
: generate N completions independently at temperature > 0 and select the one with highest mean logprob.
Named-entity tokens (names, numbers, URLs) with anomalously low logprob are higher-risk candidates to verify against external sources.
Generate the same content twice and flag responses where the per-token distributions differ substantially — a sign the content is 'unstable'.
When logprobs are the wrong tool
When the provider does not expose logprobs (Anthropic Claude, some Gemini deployments). In this case, use self-consistency voting or LLM-as-judge as a substitute.
rather than generation uncertainty. Logprobs measure the model's internal confidence, not correctness — a model trained on incorrect data can produce high logprobs for wrong facts.
When the task requires uncertainty over
space rather than token space — logprobs do not capture semantic equivalence between paraphrases.
The following implementation uses the OpenAI Python SDK. The key parameters are
(return the logprob for each generated token) and
(return the N highest-probability alternative tokens at each position, where N must be between 0 and 20).

from openai import OpenAI
import math

client = OpenAI()

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Name the capital of France in one word."}],
    max_tokens=5,
    logprobs=True,
    top_logprobs=5,
    temperature=0,  # use 0 for deterministic, interpretable logprobs
)

choice = response.choices[0]
print("Generated text:", choice.message.content)
print()

# Inspect per-token logprobs
for token_info in choice.logprobs.content:
    prob_pct = round(math.exp(token_info.logprob) * 100, 1)
    print(f"  Token: {token_info.token!r:15s}  logprob: {token_info.logprob:7.3f}  prob: {prob_pct}%")
    # Show alternatives at this position
    for alt in token_info.top_logprobs:
        alt_prob = round(math.exp(alt.logprob) * 100, 2)
        print(f"    alt: {alt.token!r:12s}  logprob: {alt.logprob:7.3f}  prob: {alt_prob}%")
    print()
list contains one entry per generated token. Each entry exposes:
(the top-N alternatives at this position). Iterating over these reveals not just what the model said, but how confident it was and what it nearly said instead.

import math
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def score_completion(prompt: str, completion: str, model: str = "gpt-4o-mini") -> dict:
    """Score a completion by computing mean logprob and perplexity."""
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": completion},
        ],
        max_tokens=0,   # echo mode: no new tokens generated, score supplied text
        logprobs=True,
        temperature=0,
    )
    token_logprobs = [t.logprob for t in response.choices[0].logprobs.content]
    n = len(token_logprobs)
    if n == 0:
        return {"n_tokens": 0, "total_logprob": None, "mean_logprob": None, "perplexity": None}
    total = sum(token_logprobs)
    mean_lp = total / n
    perplexity = math.exp(-mean_lp)
    return {
        "n_tokens": n,
        "total_logprob": round(total, 4),
        "mean_logprob": round(mean_lp, 4),
        "perplexity": round(perplexity, 3),
    }

question = "What is the boiling point of water at sea level?"
answers = [
    "100 degrees Celsius, or 212 degrees Fahrenheit.",                          # correct, expected
    "It depends greatly on factors like altitude and barometric pressure.",      # hedged, unexpected
    "The Eiffel Tower was built in 1889.",                                       # irrelevant
]

for ans in answers:
    score = score_completion(question, ans)
    print(f"Answer: {ans[:55]!r}...")
    print(f"  Mean logprob: {score['mean_logprob']}  Perplexity: {score['perplexity']}")
    print()

The factually accurate answer scores the lowest perplexity (highest mean logprob) because the model found its tokens most predictable given the question. Best-of-N selection uses this to pick the most coherent of several sampled candidates.
Zero-shot text classification via logprob comparison

import math
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are a sentiment classifier. "
    "Classify the user text with exactly one word: Positive, Neutral, or Negative."
)

def classify_sentiment(text: str) -> dict:
    """
    Score each label as a completion and pick the highest-scoring one.
    No generation needed — we compare label probabilities directly.
    """
    labels = ["Positive", "Neutral", "Negative"]
    scores: dict[str, float] = {}

    for label in labels:
        resp = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o-mini",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
                {"role": "user", "content": text},
                {"role": "assistant", "content": label},
            ],
            max_tokens=0,
            logprobs=True,
            temperature=0,
        )
        # Sum logprobs for the label's tokens (some labels are one token, some two)
        lp_sum = sum(t.logprob for t in resp.choices[0].logprobs.content)
        scores[label] = lp_sum

    best_label = max(scores, key=scores.__getitem__)
    # Convert to relative probabilities using softmax over the scores
    max_score = max(scores.values())
    exp_scores = {k: math.exp(v - max_score) for k, v in scores.items()}
    total = sum(exp_scores.values())
    probs = {k: round(v / total * 100, 1) for k, v in exp_scores.items()}

    return {"label": best_label, "probabilities": probs}

tests = [
    "I absolutely love this product — it exceeded all my expectations!",
    "The package arrived on Tuesday as scheduled.",
    "This is the worst customer service I have ever experienced.",
]
for t in tests:
    result = classify_sentiment(t)
    print(f"Text: {t[:50]!r}...")
    print(f"  Label: {result['label']}  Probs: {result['probabilities']}")
    print()

This pattern is called logprob-based classification or calibrated label scoring. It requires no fine-tuning, no output parsing, and no regex — the model never generates text. The developer computes which label the model assigns the highest log-probability as a completion.

A document-question-answering service receives user questions and generates answers from retrieved context. Before returning any answer, an orchestrator inspects the generation's mean logprob to decide whether to surface the answer directly or route it to a slower, more expensive verification step.

import math
from dataclasses import dataclass
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

@dataclass
class AnswerResult:
    answer: str
    mean_logprob: float
    perplexity: float
    confidence: str           # 'high', 'medium', 'low'
    route: str                # 'direct', 'verify', 'escalate'

# Calibrated on a held-out eval set for this domain
HIGH_THRESHOLD   = -0.4   # mean logprob above this → direct to user
MEDIUM_THRESHOLD = -1.2   # between HIGH and MEDIUM → run retrieval verification
                           # below MEDIUM → escalate to human review

def answer_question(question: str, context: str) -> AnswerResult:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "Answer the question using only the provided context. "
                    "If the answer is not in the context, respond with exactly: "
                    "'I do not have enough information to answer this question.'"
                ),
            },
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Context:
{context}

Question: {question}"},
        ],
        max_tokens=200,
        logprobs=True,
        temperature=0,
    )
    choice = resp.choices[0]
    lps = [t.logprob for t in choice.logprobs.content]
    mean_lp = sum(lps) / len(lps) if lps else -999.0
    perplexity = math.exp(-mean_lp)

    if mean_lp >= HIGH_THRESHOLD:
        confidence, route = "high", "direct"
    elif mean_lp >= MEDIUM_THRESHOLD:
        confidence, route = "medium", "verify"
    else:
        confidence, route = "low", "escalate"

    return AnswerResult(
        answer=choice.message.content,
        mean_logprob=round(mean_lp, 4),
        perplexity=round(perplexity, 3),
        confidence=confidence,
        route=route,
    )

# --- Demo ---
ctx = "The Eiffel Tower was constructed between 1887 and 1889 and stands 330 metres tall."
result = answer_question("When was the Eiffel Tower completed?", ctx)
print(f"Answer:     {result.answer}")
print(f"Mean LP:    {result.mean_logprob}  Perplexity: {result.perplexity}")
print(f"Confidence: {result.confidence}  Route: {result.route}")
if result.route == "verify":
    print("[PIPELINE]: Routing to retrieval-augmented fact check...")
elif result.route == "escalate":
    print("[PIPELINE]: Escalating to human reviewer.")

In a production deployment, the threshold values are tuned empirically on a labelled evaluation set. Teams typically plot mean logprob versus answer accuracy and set thresholds at the inflection points that achieve the desired precision-recall trade-off on the routing decision.
Applying a probability-style threshold directly to a logprob
Logprobs are always negative or zero. Comparing a logprob to a threshold like
(as if it were a probability) is always false and silently lets every output through. The correct threshold for 'at least 70 % probability' is
Summing logprobs of different-length sequences without normalizing

A 50-token completion almost always has a lower total logprob than a 5-token completion simply because more terms contribute negative values. Comparing generation confidence between outputs of different lengths requires normalizing by token count — use mean logprob or perplexity, not the raw sum.
Treating low logprob as proof of hallucination

Low logprob means the model was uncertain at the token level; it does not identify hallucinated content. A model can assign high logprobs to a hallucinated fact that was common in training data. Logprobs are a useful
for hallucination, not a classifier. Combine them with retrieval-augmented verification for critical facts.
Logprobs are specific to a model's vocabulary size, architecture, and training distribution. A score of
from GPT-4o is not directly comparable to
from a different model with a different tokenizer. Cross-model comparisons require normalizing to a common benchmark or using model-agnostic metrics.
Requesting logprobs at temperature > 1 for confidence signals

At temperature > 1 the distribution is artificially flattened, making all logprobs more negative than they would be at temperature 0. This makes the raw logprob value a poor confidence indicator. For uncertainty quantification, always generate at temperature 0 or 1 to get the model's unscaled distribution.
When comparing confidence across outputs of different lengths, always use mean logprob or perplexity — never compare raw totals.
Temperature > 0 introduces noise that makes logprob values harder to interpret as calibrated confidence estimates.

When mean logprob is poor, examine which positions dragged it down and what the alternatives were. This distinguishes genuine ambiguity (two competing plausible tokens) from out-of-distribution inputs (no tokens had high probability).
Do not set confidence thresholds by intuition. Measure accuracy versus mean logprob on a representative evaluation set and set thresholds at the inflection point.
For pipelines that may need retrospective analysis of failures, log the raw per-token logprob data with each response. Re-running the query incurs cost and may not produce identical results.
is the natural logarithm, consistent with the API's convention.
produces different values and breaks comparison arithmetic.
In best-of-N workflows, generate all N completions first in parallel, then score them — rather than scoring during generation. This maximizes API throughput.

Requesting logprobs does not materially increase inference latency. The log-softmax over the full vocabulary is computed as part of every forward pass; the overhead is packaging the per-token data in the response. The added network transfer scales as
is the number of output tokens and
this is 10,000 logprob entries per response — manageable but worth budgeting for in memory-constrained environments.
trick to score pre-generated candidates in batch. Separate generation (expensive) from scoring (cheap) to parallelize effectively.
to score N candidates concurrently within a single rate-limit window.
Local hosted models for high-volume scoring.
Running vLLM or llama.cpp locally avoids per-token API costs for high-volume logprob-based ranking. vLLM exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint with

INT8 or INT4 quantized variants of hosted models produce logprobs very close to the full-precision model at a fraction of the memory and compute cost, suitable for real-time confidence gating with limited GPU budget.
The memory footprint of the returned data is dominated by the
returns only the chosen token's logprob — the minimum data needed for sequence-level confidence scoring.
Full per-token support in Chat Completions
Structured ChoiceLogprobs objects with token, logprob, top_logprobs
No logprob surface; use Claude for generation, not logprob-based scoring
Full logit tensor; apply F.log_softmax(logits, dim=-1) manually
Access logprob metadata through LLMResult response_metadata
Pass through OpenAI client parameters via llm.complete with extra kwargs
For Hugging Face models, the full vocabulary logit tensor is always available:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2").eval()

def get_token_logprobs(text: str) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
    """Return [(token_str, logprob), ...] for each token in text."""
    ids = tokenizer.encode(text, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        logits = model(ids).logits          # (1, seq_len, vocab_size)
    log_probs = F.log_softmax(logits[0], dim=-1)  # (seq_len, vocab_size)

    results = []
    for i in range(1, ids.shape[1]):
        tok_id = ids[0, i].item()
        lp = log_probs[i - 1, tok_id].item()   # position i-1 predicts token at i
        results.append((tokenizer.decode([tok_id]), round(lp, 4)))
    return results

for tok, lp in get_token_logprobs("The cat sat on the mat."):
    print(f"{tok!r:12s} logprob={lp:7.4f}  prob={round(torch.exp(torch.tensor(lp)).item()*100,1)}%")
Several techniques address related goals — output confidence, uncertainty estimation, and candidate ranking — with different requirements and accuracy trade-offs:
Semantic proximity of query and response
Whether answer is grounded in retrieved docs

: available at generation time, with no extra model calls. Self-consistency and LLM-as-judge provide richer quality signals but at significantly higher token cost. Production systems commonly combine logprob gating (first pass) with retrieval verification or LLM-as-judge (second pass) only for outputs flagged by the logprob filter.

Verbalized confidence — prompting the model to state its own confidence — is poorly calibrated in practice: models systematically overstate certainty. Logprobs reflect the underlying distribution directly and are generally better calibrated than verbalized estimates for generation uncertainty (though neither is calibrated for factual accuracy).
A prompt injection that causes the model to generate confident-sounding incorrect content will produce high logprobs. Do not use logprob confidence as a sole security gate for high-stakes actions.

A model's logprob calibration reflects its training distribution. If runtime inputs are substantially out-of-distribution (unfamiliar domain, language, or topic), logprob values can be poorly calibrated — the model may be highly confident and wrong. Monitor accuracy versus mean logprob on held-out domain-specific data.

When a provider updates a model (e.g. gpt-4o-mini-2024-10-18 to a later version), the logprob distribution can shift. Thresholds calibrated on one version may need recalibration for the next. Pin model versions in production and re-evaluate thresholds on each upgrade.

exposes the model's distribution over all high-probability tokens at each position. In applications where the system prompt is confidential, the top alternatives might leak information about prompt content. Do not surface raw logprob data to end users in such systems.

When logprobs gate agentic actions (e.g. 'only execute code if mean logprob > threshold'), use conservative thresholds and treat logprob as one signal among several. Always combine with hard limits, sandboxed execution environments, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints for actions with real-world consequences.
Think of the model's vocabulary as a ballot with 50,000+ candidate tokens. At each generation step, the model allocates votes across all candidates based on the context. The
are the raw vote tallies (unbounded real numbers). The
converts tallies into vote shares that sum to 1. The
is the logarithm of the vote share for each token.

Because the logarithm is monotonically increasing, the highest-logprob token is always the same as the highest-probability token — they rank identically. The value itself tells you how dominant that token was: logprob 0 means 100 % of the vote; logprob −0.69 means about 50 %; logprob −4.6 means about 1 %.
Summing logprobs across a sequence is equivalent to multiplying their probabilities — but without floating-point underflow. When probabilities are small (

per token is common), multiplying 200 of them together produces a number so small that 64-bit floats round it to zero. Summing their logarithms instead keeps the arithmetic in a safe range. This is the fundamental reason the entire field of statistical language modeling works in log-space: products become sums, and tiny numbers become manageable negative ones.
Logprobs are the natural logarithm of per-token softmax probabilities. They are always
), with values closer to zero indicating higher model confidence.
The pipeline is: tokenize → forward pass → logits → log-softmax → logprob. Each position's logprob reflects the model's distribution at that step given all preceding context.
when comparing confidence across sequences of different lengths. Use mean logprob or perplexity — never compare raw sums from differently-sized outputs.
without fine-tuning: compare the logprob of each label completion given a shared prompt prefix and pick the highest.
. A model can produce high logprobs for hallucinated content that was common in training. Use logprobs for uncertainty gating, and retrieval or LLM-as-judge for factual verification.
Provider support varies: OpenAI exposes logprobs via
; Anthropic Claude does not. For local models, use
In production agentic systems, logprob gating is a
that should be layered with self-consistency or retrieval verification for high-stakes outputs.

Calibrate thresholds empirically on held-out evaluation data, re-evaluate on model version upgrades, and be aware of distribution shift when deploying in domains different from the model's training distribution.
